concepts.title: Combinations
concepts.conceptBody:
Combinations

Each of different selections made by taking all or some numbers out of defined objects, irrespective of their arrangement is called combination.

Example:

In how many ways can be select 2 teams out of 4 to play for the final. Let the teams be India, Australia, Pakistan & England. We know we have to select 2 teams, let us see the possible selection.

India vs Australia $-1$

India vs Pakistan $-2$

India vs England $-3$

Australia vs Pakistan - 4

Australia vs England – 5

Pakistan vs England - 6

Other already covered cases

Pakistan vs India $-$ same as 2

Pakistan vs Australia - same as 4

England vs India $-$ same as 3

England vs Australia - same as 5

England vs Pakistan $-$ same as 6

$\therefore $ Total selections possible $=6$

Again note, while selection ORDER is NOT IMPORTANT. So, combinations ABC, ACB, CAB, $\text{CBA},\text{BAC}~\And~ \text{BCA}$ are equal to 1 combination i.e. $\text{ABC}$, whereas they are 6 different permutations.

Notation: 

Combinations of $\text{n}$ different things taken $\text{r}$ at a time.

$={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}\text{ or }\text{ C}\left( \text{n},\text{r} \right)\text{ or }\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{n} \\ \text{r} \\ \end{array} \right)=\frac{\text{n}!}{\text{r}!\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)!}$

Properties of ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}$

1. ${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}=1$

2. ${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}-\text{r}}}$

3.${{}^{n}}{{C}_{r}}+{{}^{n}}{{C}_{r-1}}={{}^{n+1}}{{C}_{r}}$

4.${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}=\left( \frac{\text{n}}{\text{r}} \right){{}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}$

5. $\frac{{{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}}{{{}^{\text{n}-1}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}-1}}}=\left( \frac{\text{n}}{\text{n}-\text{r}+1} \right)$

6. if ${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{x}}}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{y}}}$, then $\text{x}=\text{y}$ or $\text{x}+\text{y}=\text{n}$

7. $\frac{{{}^{\text{n}+1}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}+1}}}{{{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}}=\left( \frac{\text{n}+1}{\text{r}+1} \right)$

8. The greatest value of ${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}$, when

$\text{n}$ is even, is ${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}/2}}$

$\text{n}$ is odd, is $\frac{{{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}}}{2}$ or ${{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\frac{\text{n}-1}{2}}}$

Q) There are 10 candidates standing from your area in election. You all given the liberty of giving votes to any 2 of them. In how many ways can you vote? 

Solution: 

You have to select any 2 candidates from 10 where the order in which you choose does not matter. Hence selecting 2 out of 10.

$={{}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}=\frac{10!}{2!8!}=45\text{ selections }\text{ possible}\text{. }$

Q) Suppose that IIT paper is divided into paper $1\And $ paper 2 each having 10 questions. You somehow know that cut off to clear the paper is to rightly attempt 5 questions & 7 questions, in paper $1\And $ paper 2 respectively. So, in how many ways you can clear IIT? 

Solution: 

See here also order does not matter, in fact the number of correct answers is the key. So total ways of clearing IIT.

= Selecting 5 questions from paper 1 selecting 7 questions from paper II

$={{10}_{{{\text{C}}_{5}}}}\times {{10}_{{{\text{C}}_{7}}}}$

Q) In how many ways a committee of 6 members can be formed from 6 men and 6 women, consisting of 4 men and 2 women? 

Solution: 

You have to select 4 men from a total of 6 men and 2 women from 6, which could be done in ${{~}^{6}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{6}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$ ways.

$\therefore$ Number of ways in which committee can be formed

= 15 $15=225$ (also note that ${ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_2$)

Q) If you invite 10 of your friends to party and all of you shake hands with each other, then how many shake hands happen in the party? 

Solution: 

Note that when you shake hands it is immaterial that in which order you shake hands. When 2 persons shake hands, it is counted as one shake hand only.

$\therefore$ Total number of shake hands

= number of ways of selecting 2 of your friends from 10

$={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}=45$

Q) (a) How many lines can be drawn through $\text{n}$ points? 

(b) How many diagonals are there in a polygon with $\text{n}$ sides? 

Solution: 

(a) What is a line? How it is formed? Answer to these simple questions can solve this part for you.

A line is formed by joining 2 points on a plane. So, for the formation of line we need 2 points.

$\therefore$ Number of lines possible when $\text{n}$ points are there $={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$

(b) Taking of diagonals, they are also lines. Let us understand this by taking a small example first.

Total lines through $\text{n}$ points $={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$ lines used in making the polynomial of $\text{n}$ sides $=\text{n}$

(you can check this that, quadrilateral $=4$ sides, pentagon $=5$ etc)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Remaining lines must be diagonals

$={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}-\text{n}=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}{2}-\text{n}=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}-3 \right)}{2}$

Q) In how many ways can a cricket team be selected from a group of 25 players containing 10 batsmen, 8 bowlers, 5 all-rounders and 2 wicket keepers? Assume that the team of 11 players India will be playing with will have 4 batsmen, 3 all-rounders, 3 bowlers and 1 wicket keeper? 

Solution: 

This is a very simple problem, we just have to choose from the available options for the different positions.

  1. Selection of batsmen $-{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}$
  2. Selection of bowler $-{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$
  3. Selection of all rounders $-{{~}^{5}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$
  4. Selection of wicket keeper $-{{~}^{2}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The team will be formed by combination of all these selections

$={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{8}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{5}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{2}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ways }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Q) (a) How many triangles can be formed through n points, given that no 3 points are collinear. 

(b) How many triangles can be formed out of a Hexagon? 

Solution: 

(a) Every problem can be understood if we try to break it down to a simpler & easy problem. For a triangle we need three points which are non-collinear (remember this condition is important). 
 

triangles possible when 4 points are there or ${{~}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}=4$

$\therefore$ similarly, for $\text{n}$ points it would be ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$.

Similarly, we can extend this for rn-sided polygon.

$={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{m}}}\left( \text{m}\le \text{n} \right)$

(b) Hexagon is formed by six points, and for triangle we need to select any three of them at a time. Hence number of triangles $={{~}^{6}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}=20$

Q) If m parallel lines in a plane are intersected by a family of n parallel lines. Find the number of parallelograms formed. 

Solution: 

If you are not able to understand the problem, break the problem.

We can see that for parallelogram we need two pair of straight lines.

And from the question we have $\text{m}n$ parallel lines So for a parallelogram to exist we just need to select 2 straight lines from each set of parallel lines. This can be done in

$={ }^{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{C}_2 \quad{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_2$, ways

$=\frac{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{m}-1)}{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}$

$=\frac{m n(m-1)(n-1)}{4}$

Q) There are 10 points in a plane, no three of which are in the same straight line, except 4 points which are collinear. Find 

(i) number of straight lines obtained from the pairs of these points. 

(ii) number of triangles that can be formed with the vertices as these points. 

Solution: 

This question is different from the earlier ones as there are collinear points here.

(i) for number of lines, total number of lines if all are not collinear $={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\ldots \left( \text{i} \right)$

So, lines from 4 points $={{~}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}$

$\And $ now we need to subtract this from (i), as these 4 points are collinear

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total lines $={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}-{{~}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+1=40$

{Why +1, we removed all lines made by 4 collinear points but they will form at least & at most 1 line containing all those 4 points. So, we have to add that line too}

(ii) Here also we will apply the same procedure as followed in above part

Number of triangles possible joining 10 points taken 3 at a time $={{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}=120$

But 4 points are collinear, otherwise if they had not been collinear then number of triangles $={{~}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}=4$

So now we have to remove this from total selections $=120-4=116$

(We subtracted the value ${{~}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$ as there is no triangle possible through collinear points)

TIP: Though there are formulas for both permutations and combinations for solving a question, we advise you not to use that many formulas and to further eliminate confusion between which formula to be used we will solve each question through combinations. In this way you will never require to learn permutations formula.

For arrangement we will first select the required result and arrange them rather than using formula.
